title: Requirement Defect Distribution
content: 56% Incomplete Requirements, 26% Ambiguous Language, 12% Missing Edge Cases, 6% Conflicting Requirements. KEY INSIGHT: 82% of requirement defects are preventable with proper writing techniques and structured reviews.
tags: defect distribution root cause prevention quality

title: Cost of Ambiguity by Stage
content: Discovery stage cost multipliers — Requirements: 1x, Design: 5x, Development: 10x, Testing: 20x, Production: 100x+. Fix requirements early to minimize cost.
tags: cost ambiguity stage discovery prevention economics

title: Requirement Specification Template
content: Sections: IDENTIFICATION (REQ-ID, Title, Version, Status), DESCRIPTION (EARS Pattern: WHEN trigger the system SHALL action SO THAT value), ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (Gherkin: Given/When/Then for happy path + edge cases), METADATA (Priority MoSCoW, Source, Author), TRACEABILITY (Parent, Children, Dependencies, Conflicts), NOTES (Rationale, Assumptions, Open Questions).
tags: template specification structure format sections

title: Requirement ID Convention
content: BR=Business Requirement, SR=Stakeholder Requirement, FR=Functional (FR-AUTH-001), NFR=Non-Functional (NFR-PERF-001), UC=Use Case, US=User Story, BR-DATA=Business Rule Data, BR-PROC=Business Rule Process, INT=Integration (INT-SAP-001). Format: [Category]-[Module]-[Number].
tags: naming convention id format categorization

title: EARS Patterns for Requirements
content: 6 patterns: (1) UBIQUITOUS — The system SHALL action. (2) EVENT-DRIVEN — WHEN trigger, the system SHALL action. (3) STATE-DRIVEN — WHILE state, the system SHALL action. (4) OPTIONAL — WHERE feature is included, the system SHALL action. (5) UNWANTED — IF condition, THEN the system SHALL action. (6) COMPLEX — WHILE state, WHEN trigger, the system SHALL action.
tags: ears pattern syntax unambiguous functional requirement template

title: Ambiguity Detection Word List
content: ALWAYS scan for these forbidden words and replace with testable metrics: adequate, appropriate, as quickly as possible, easy, efficient, fast, flexible, good, intuitive, lightweight, maximize, minimize, normal, optimal, quick, reasonable, robust, seamless, simple, sufficient, timely, transparent, user-friendly, TBD. Fix pattern: Replace with metric + threshold + measurement method.
tags: ambiguous forbidden words vague testable metric detection scan

title: SRS Functional Requirement Sentence Pattern
content: Pattern: [restriction] subject action-verb [observable result] [qualifier]. Where: restriction = condition (When approved / If no contractor), subject = The system or actor, action verb = task performed, observable result = user-visible outcome, qualifier = quality constraint (within 3 seconds). Good: The system shall allow a scheduler to select services. Bad: The system should handle jobs efficiently (ambiguous!).
tags: srs functional requirement sentence pattern template formula

title: Feature to FR Hierarchy Pattern
content: Decompose features into functional requirements using hierarchy: FEATURE: Schedule Jobs → FR-SCH-001: Display available time slots → FR-SCH-002: Allow scheduler to select services → FR-SCH-003: When contractor unavailable suggest alternatives → FR-SCH-004: Send confirmation within 60 seconds. Use continuance patterns (below / as follows / following) to link feature to FRs.
tags: hierarchy decomposition feature functional requirement traceability

title: Gherkin Acceptance Criteria Format
content: Behavioral specification using Given/When/Then: Scenario 1 (Happy Path) — GIVEN precondition WHEN action THEN expected result AND additional result. Scenario 2 (Edge Case) — GIVEN precondition WHEN action THEN expected result. Each user story needs minimum 2 scenarios: happy path + error/edge case. Criteria must be testable and automatable.
tags: gherkin bdd acceptance criteria given when then scenario testable

title: INVEST Criteria for User Stories
content: I=Independent (no dependency on other stories), N=Negotiable (not a contract, open to discussion), V=Valuable (delivers user/business value), E=Estimable (team can size it), S=Small (fits in one sprint), T=Testable (clear acceptance criteria). A story failing any criterion should be rewritten or split.
tags: invest user story quality criteria check agile

title: Three Levels of Requirements
content: Level 1: BUSINESS Requirements — Why are we doing this? (Goals, objectives, vision). Level 2: USER Requirements — What do users need to do? (Use cases, user stories, scenarios). Level 3: SOFTWARE Requirements — How does the system behave? (Functional specs, NFRs, interfaces). Each level traces to the one above. Orphan requirements at any level indicate gold plating or missing context.
tags: levels hierarchy business user software traceability

title: Vision Statement Template
content: Structure: FOR [target customer/user] WHO [statement of need or opportunity] THE [product name] IS [product category] THAT [key benefit compelling reason] UNLIKE [primary competitive alternative] OUR PRODUCT [statement of primary differentiation]. Step 1: Answer each line. Step 2: Combine into one paragraph. Step 3: Circulate for stakeholder revision.
tags: vision statement template product scope strategy

title: Passive Voice Detection
content: Requirements using passive voice hide the actor and create ambiguity. Bad: Data is validated (By whom? The system? The user?). Bad: The report is generated (When? By what trigger?). Fix: Identify the actor explicitly. Good: The system SHALL validate the input data. Good: WHEN the user clicks Export, the system SHALL generate the report. Always scan for is/are/was/were + past participle patterns.
tags: passive voice actor ambiguity detection grammar

title: Decision Table for Complex Logic
content: Use when: 4+ conditions with multiple outcomes, complex business rules with exceptions, pricing/discount/eligibility rules. Structure: Conditions (rows) x Rules (columns) matrix. Each column = one rule combination. Actions row shows outcome for that combination. Ensures completeness — 2^N combinations for N binary conditions. Catches missing rules that prose requirements miss.
tags: decision table logic complex business rules conditions

title: RFC 2119 Keyword Standards
content: SHALL/MUST = absolute requirement (mandatory). SHALL NOT/MUST NOT = absolute prohibition. SHOULD = recommended but exceptions allowed with justification. SHOULD NOT = not recommended but permitted with justification. MAY = truly optional. Use consistently throughout specifications. Never use: will, might, can, could (ambiguous obligation level).
tags: rfc 2119 keywords shall must should may obligation

title: SRS Inspection Checklist
content: Correctness: Solution-independent? Free from factual errors? Cross-references correct? Clarity: Single interpretation only? Uniquely identified? Consistent detail level? Completeness: All interfaces defined? All inputs/outputs specified? All business rules documented? Quality attributes have metrics? Consistency: No conflicts between requirements? Trade-offs specified? Relevancy: Each requirement necessary? Traceable to origin? Feasibility: Achievable with current tech? Within resources? Verifiability: Can be tested? Test criteria derivable?
tags: inspection checklist srs quality review verification

title: Requirements Development Lifecycle
content: 6 steps: (1) Define Vision — stakeholders + scope + vision statement. (2) Develop Models — context diagram + event-response + data models. (3) Analyze Requirements — functional + non-functional + business rules. (4) Specify Requirements — SRS/URD document using templates. (5) Validate Requirements — peer review + UAT + prototype. (6) Manage Requirements — baseline + change control + traceability. Iterative: steps 2-5 repeat as understanding deepens.
tags: lifecycle development process steps iterative

title: User Story Format
content: As a [role/persona], I want to [action/goal], so that [benefit/value]. Quality checks: Role is specific (not just 'user'). Action is concrete and achievable. Benefit traces to business value. Each story has acceptance criteria in Gherkin. Story fits INVEST criteria. Max 1-2 sprints to implement.
tags: user story format role action benefit agile

title: Quality Attributes Taxonomy
content: Operational: Performance (response time, concurrent users), Reliability (MTBF, failure rate), Robustness (restart time, failure events%), Security (unauthorized attempts blocked%), Usability (time to competence, error rate). Deployment: Availability (% uptime), Scalability (user growth range), Portability (migration cost), Recoverability (time to restore). Development: Maintainability (time to fix), Testability (cost per defect), Reusability (integration cost). Each attribute needs: Tag, Scale, Meter, Must, Plan, Wish (Planguage).
tags: quality attributes nfr iso 25010 planguage taxonomy operational

title: 4W+H Requirements Analysis Framework
content: WHO → Actor models (stakeholders, personas, roles). WHAT → Data models (ERD, data dictionary, glossary). WHEN → State/Event models (state diagrams, event-response tables). WHY → Business rules and policies (decision tables, constraint rules). HOW → Process/Use case models (BPMN, activity diagrams, scenarios). Each W maps to specific analysis models. Complete coverage = all 5 questions answered with appropriate models.
tags: 4w1h analysis framework models who what when why how

title: Decision Table When-to-Use Guide
content: Use Decision Table WHEN: 4+ conditions with multiple outcomes (essential); complex business rules with exceptions (essential); pricing/discount/eligibility rules (essential). Skip for: simple if-then rule (natural language fine); 2-3 conditions with 2-3 outcomes (optional). Key value: reveals missing rule combinations that prose requirements miss.
tags: decision table complex logic business rules conditions matrix

title: Decision Table Structure Template
content: Structure: Rule Name + Purpose header; COLUMNS = Rules (Rule 1..N); ROWS = Conditions then Actions. Condition cells: Y=Yes/N=No/-=Don't care. Action cells: X=Execute. Legend: Y/N/-/X. For N binary conditions: 2^N possible combinations to check. Each column = one rule combination. Ensures completeness — all paths covered.
tags: decision table template structure conditions actions rules completeness

title: Decision Table Discount Eligibility Example
content: Gold Member + Order>$100 → 20% discount + loyalty email. Gold Member + Order<=100 → 10% discount + loyalty email. NOT Gold + Order>$100 → 10% discount. NOT Gold + first purchase → 5% discount. NOT Gold + NOT first + Order<=100 → no discount. Generates 5 EARS-style FRs: FR-DISC-001 to FR-DISC-005.
tags: decision table example discount gold member eligibility pricing

title: State Diagram When-to-Use Guide
content: Use State Diagrams WHEN: entity has distinct statuses/states; transitions between states have rules; not all transitions are allowed; actions depend on current state. Common examples: Order lifecycle (Draft→Submitted→Approved→Shipped→Delivered); User account (Active→Suspended→Deactivated); Document workflow (Draft→Review→Approved→Published); Ticket/Issue (Open→InProgress→Resolved→Closed).
tags: state diagram lifecycle status workflow entity transitions

title: State Transition Table Template
content: Columns: Current State | Trigger/Event | Next State | Guard Condition. Example Order: DRAFT+Submit→PENDING_APPR (Guard: all required fields set); PENDING_APPR+Approve→APPROVED (Guard: approver has authority); APPROVED+Cancel→CANCELLED (Guard: within 24 hours); PAID+Ship→SHIPPED (Guard: inventory available). Each row generates one functional requirement.
tags: state transition table template guard condition lifecycle

title: Requirements from State Transitions
content: Convert state transition table rows to EARS requirements: FR-ORD-010: System SHALL allow order submission only when all required fields populated. FR-ORD-011: System SHALL allow order cancellation within 24 hours of approval. FR-ORD-012: System SHALL prevent state transitions not defined in the State Transition Table. Rule: one FR per transition row.
tags: state transition requirements generation ears pattern functional

title: Specification by Example (SBE) Process
content: 5 steps: (1) Derive Scope from Goals — min viable scope; (2) Specify Collaboratively — Three Amigos (BA+Dev+QA) with What-if scenarios; (3) Illustrate with Examples — happy path + edge cases + error scenarios + real data; (4) Refine to Executable Specification — Gherkin scenarios + data tables as living docs; (5) Automate Tests — Cucumber/SpecFlow/Behave.
tags: sbe specification example three amigos bdd living documentation

title: Gherkin Scenario Outline with Data Tables
content: Feature: Order Discount Calculation. Background: Given discount rules configured (Gold/$0/10%; Gold/$100/20%; Silver/$50/5%; None/$100/5%). Scenario Outline: Given customer has membership AND order total; When checkout initiated; Then discount applied. Examples table: columns Membership|OrderTotal|ExpectedDiscount|FinalTotal. Runs one test per table row.
tags: gherkin scenario outline data table parameterized bdd automation

title: Functional Requirement Pattern: Validation
content: The system SHALL validate [field] as follows: [Rule 1]; [Rule 2]; [Rule 3]. WHEN validation fails the system SHALL: display error message [specific text]; highlight invalid field; prevent form submission. Example: Email — must contain exactly one @; domain must contain at least one dot; max 254 chars; must not be already registered.
tags: validation pattern functional requirement error message field

title: Functional Requirement Pattern: CRUD
content: The system SHALL allow [user role] to: CREATE [entity] with fields [list]; READ [entity] via list view and detail view; UPDATE [entity] fields [updateable list]; DELETE [entity] (soft/hard delete) with confirmation. Example: Administrator manages Product with Name/SKU/Price/Category. DELETE = soft delete with confirmation dialog.
tags: crud pattern functional requirement actor entity fields

title: Functional Requirement Pattern: Integration
content: Pattern: The system SHALL integrate with [external system] to [purpose]. Specify: Protocol (REST/SOAP/GraphQL/File); Authentication (OAuth2/API Key/Certificate); Frequency (real-time/batch/on-demand); Timeout (X seconds); Retry (N attempts with X second backoff); Error Handling (logging/alerting/fallback). Example: SAP S/4HANA REST OAuth2 real-time 30s 3-retry ELK+PagerDuty.
tags: integration pattern functional requirement protocol authentication retry

title: Functional Requirement Pattern: Notification
content: Pattern: The system SHALL send [notification type] to [recipient] WHEN [trigger] containing [information elements] via [channels] within [time constraint]. Example: Send Order Confirmation Email to Customer WHEN order placed successfully; containing Order ID/Items/Total/Estimated Delivery; via email to registered address; within 5 minutes.
tags: notification pattern functional requirement trigger channel time

title: Requirement Quality Review Checklist - Content
content: CONTENT QUALITY: Uses SHALL/SHOULD/MAY correctly and consistently; No ambiguous words (fast/easy/user-friendly); All terms defined or in glossary; Specific and measurable where applicable; Includes error handling and edge cases; Has acceptance criteria (Given-When-Then or checklist); Examples provided for complex requirements.
tags: content quality checklist review ambiguous terms acceptance criteria

title: Requirement Quality Review Checklist - Structure
content: STRUCTURE: Unique meaningful ID assigned; Clear concise title (max 10 words); Priority assigned (MoSCoW or numeric); Source documented (who/when/where); Version and status current; Dependencies identified; Traced to parent requirement or business goal. RED FLAGS: no AND joining unrelated concepts; no passive voice without actor; no TBDs; no implementation details.
tags: structure checklist review id priority source traceability

title: Requirement Quality Attributes Checklist
content: 8 quality attributes to check per requirement: Atomic (one requirement per statement); Complete (all info to implement is present); Consistent (no conflicts with other requirements); Correct (accurately represents stakeholder need); Feasible (can be implemented within constraints); Necessary (traces to business need not gold plating); Unambiguous (only one interpretation); Verifiable (can be tested/measured).
tags: quality attributes atomic complete consistent correct feasible testable

title: Extended Requirement Quality Criteria (INVEST+ACC+U)
content: Extended INVEST for requirements: Independent (implement alone); Negotiable (open to discussion); Valuable (delivers value); Estimable (effort can be estimated); Small (fits one sprint); Testable (acceptance verifiable); Atomic (one req per statement); Complete (all info present); Consistent (no conflicts with others); Unambiguous (one interpretation only).
tags: invest extended quality criteria checklist requirement agile

title: Ambiguous Word Replacement Reference
content: Fast/Quick → Within 2 seconds 95th percentile. User-friendly → WCAG 2.1 AA compliant or 3 clicks max. Easy → First-time user completes in less than 5 min. Appropriate → Specify exact criteria. Flexible → Supports configuration of X/Y/Z. Robust → Recovers from failure within 30s. Seamless → No visible delay during transition. Real-time → Within 500ms of event. Efficient → Use less than 100MB RAM less than 5% CPU.
tags: ambiguous word replacement fix testable metric measurable

title: Requirements Quality Metrics
content: 7 metrics: Volatility = Changed/Total x100 (target <15%); Completeness = Approved/Identified x100 (>95%); Testability = Verifiable/Total x100 (100%); Traceability = Traced/Total x100 (100%); Defect Rate = Defects Found/Total Reqs (<0.5); Approval Cycle = days Draft to Approved (<5 days); TBD Resolution = Resolved TBDs/Total TBDs x100 (100% before baseline).
tags: metrics quality volatility completeness testability traceability defect rate

title: CMMI Level 4 Requirements Quality Baselines
content: Defects per 100 Reqs: World-Class <1; Acceptable 1-3; Needs Improvement >3. Requirements Volatility: World-Class <10%; Acceptable 10-20%. Traceability Coverage: World-Class >98%; Acceptable 95-98%. First-Pass Approval: World-Class >90%; Acceptable 85-90%. Review Effectiveness: World-Class >85%; Acceptable 80-85%. Process control: UCL=Mean+3sigma; LCL=Mean-3sigma; investigate if outside limits.
tags: cmmi quality baseline defect density volatility traceability first-pass

title: Process Capability Index (Cpk) for Requirements
content: Cpk = min[(UCL - Mean) / 3sigma ; (Mean - LCL) / 3sigma]. Cpk >= 1.33: Process is capable (CMMI Level 4 target). Cpk 1.0-1.33: Marginally capable. Cpk < 1.0: Needs improvement. Track Defect Density monthly. Control Chart: UCL=4.5; LCL=0; Mean=2.0 (example). Investigate root cause if any data point falls outside control limits.
tags: cpk capability index process control defect density statistical

title: Requirements Development Lifecycle - 6 Steps Detail
content: (1) Define Vision: stakeholders + scope + vision statement. (2) Develop Models: context diagram + event-response + data models. (3) Analyze Requirements: functional + non-functional + business rules. (4) Specify Requirements: SRS/URD using templates. (5) Validate Requirements: peer review + UAT + prototype. (6) Manage Requirements: baseline + change control + traceability. Steps 2-5 iterate as understanding deepens.
tags: lifecycle 6 steps development process iterate baseline

title: Requirement Types - 7 Categories
content: BR=Business Requirement (why — goals/objectives); SR=Stakeholder Requirement (who wants what); FR=Functional Requirement (system behavior — FR-MODULE-001); NFR=Non-Functional Requirement (quality attributes — NFR-PERF-001); UC=Use Case (interaction flow); US=User Story (agile format); INT=Integration Requirement (INT-SAP-001). Each traces to the level above. Orphans indicate gold plating.
tags: requirement types categories hierarchy functional non-functional use case

title: Passive Voice Detection and Fix Pattern
content: Passive voice hides the actor. Scan for: is/are/was/were + past participle. Bad: Data is validated (by whom?); Report is generated (when? by what?). Fix: identify actor explicitly. Good: The system SHALL validate input data. Good: WHEN user clicks Export the system SHALL generate the report. Rule: every requirement must have a named actor (system or user role).
tags: passive voice detection fix actor explicit grammar requirement

title: Testability Rules for Requirements
content: A requirement IS testable if: (1) measurable metric exists (not just qualitative); (2) specific pass/fail criteria defined; (3) test cases can be written before implementation; (4) single responsibility (tests one thing); (5) expected output is deterministic. NOT testable: user-friendly; fast; robust; flexible; reasonable. Fix: add numbers; specify conditions; define acceptable range.
tags: testability rules metric measurable pass fail criteria test case

title: Common Requirement Mistakes - Anti-Patterns
content: 8 common mistakes: (1) AND-requirements — split compound statements; (2) Passive voice — identify actor; (3) TBD placeholders — resolve before baseline; (4) Implementation detail — specify WHAT not HOW; (5) Negative requirements — restate as positive; (6) Gold plating — remove requirements without business trace; (7) Ambiguous obligation — use RFC 2119 keywords; (8) No acceptance criteria — add Given/When/Then.
tags: mistakes anti-patterns compound passive tbd gold plating obligation

title: Planguage NFR Specification Format
content: Planguage attributes per quality attribute: Tag (unique name); Scale (unit of measure — ms/users/errors); Meter (how to measure — tool/method); Must (minimum acceptable threshold); Plan (expected value for this release); Wish (ideal target value). Example: Response Time — Scale: ms; Meter: Gatling load test; Must: <3000ms; Plan: <1500ms; Wish: <500ms. Never specify NFR without all 6 attributes.
tags: planguage nfr quality attribute metric threshold must plan wish

title: Vision Statement Formula
content: FOR [target customer] WHO [need or opportunity] THE [product name] IS [product category] THAT [key benefit compelling reason] UNLIKE [primary competitive alternative] OUR PRODUCT [primary differentiation]. Steps: (1) Answer each line separately; (2) Combine into one paragraph; (3) Circulate for stakeholder revision; (4) Baseline when approved. Used to define project scope and drive requirements.
tags: vision statement formula template scope alignment

title: Three Levels of Requirements Hierarchy
content: Level 1: BUSINESS Requirements — Why are we doing this (goals/objectives/vision). Level 2: USER Requirements — What users need to do (use cases/stories/scenarios). Level 3: SOFTWARE Requirements — How system behaves (functional specs/NFRs/interfaces). Rules: each level traces to one above; orphan requirements indicate gold plating or missing context; never skip a level.
tags: levels hierarchy business user software traceability orphan

title: SRS Structure Template
content: Sections: (1) Introduction: purpose/scope/definitions/references; (2) Overall Description: product perspective/functions/users/constraints/assumptions; (3) Functional Requirements: organized by use case or feature; (4) Non-Functional Requirements: performance/security/usability/reliability; (5) External Interface Requirements: UI/hardware/software/communication; (6) Appendices: glossary/index/models. One SRS template covers all FR and NFR.
tags: srs structure template sections organization functional non-functional

title: SRS Completeness Checklist
content: All interfaces defined (UI/API/data/hardware); All inputs and outputs specified; All business rules documented with decision tables if complex; Quality attributes have measurable metrics; All use cases have happy path + alt flows + error flows; Data entities have attributes and constraints; Integration points have protocol/auth/format specified; Glossary has all domain terms; No TBDs remaining before baseline.
tags: srs completeness checklist interfaces inputs outputs business rules
